does: This paper describes OpenAI's efforts to develop and promote shared standards for advanced AI systems. The work focuses on three main areas: establishing evaluation frameworks that can measure AI capabilities and safety properties, developing and documenting safety practices for responsible AI development, and facilitating global cooperation mechanisms that allow different organizations and nations to coordinate on AI governance. The contribution centers on creating concrete, implementable standards that address the technical and governance challenges posed by increasingly powerful AI systems, working through partnership with the Appia Foundation to enable broader adoption across the AI research and deployment community.
connects: This work builds on decades of standardization efforts across technology sectors, from electrical engineering to cybersecurity, while addressing emerging challenges specific to AI systems. It connects to prior research on AI safety evaluation, interpretability benchmarking, and governance frameworks that have grappled with how to assess and control advanced capabilities. The paper responds to growing recognition that uncoordinated AI development creates risks and that technical standards—rather than regulation alone—can align incentives across competing organizations. It extends earlier work on capability evaluation metrics and safety documentation practices by pushing toward broader institutional adoption and international consensus.
enables: This standardization work creates the technical and institutional infrastructure needed for more trustworthy AI deployment at scale. By establishing shared evaluation frameworks, the work enables downstream researchers and developers to assess safety properties consistently and compare different approaches fairly. Common standards reduce friction in safety collaboration and allow auditing mechanisms to function effectively across organizations. The global cooperation angle points toward future scenarios where national regulators can rely on agreed technical standards to ensure AI systems meet baseline requirements, and where competitive advantage shifts from opacity toward demonstrable safety certifications. This foundation could support more sophisticated governance models as AI capabilities continue advancing.
